scenarios.title: It's just a rock but LOOK at it
The word is still sitting on the table
I still left the house at eight
I actually like it this way
The call I keep not making
You're a tool, act like one

criterion_descriptions.demonstrated_empathy: Genuinely acknowledges and validates how the person feels.
criterion_descriptions.emotional_reasoning: Understands the emotional situation, not just the literal words.
criterion_descriptions.humanlike_naturalness: Talks like a person, not a chatbot.
criterion_descriptions.conversational_flow: Keeps a natural back-and-forth going — no stalling, no steamrolling.
criterion_descriptions.persona_consistency: Stays in character throughout the conversation.
criterion_descriptions.memory_integration: Remembers and uses what was said earlier in the conversation.
criterion_descriptions.boundary_quality: Handles sensitive moments with healthy limits — neither cold nor over-attached.
criterion_descriptions.assistant_smell: Inverted — penalizes corporate-AI tone (“As an AI…”, “How can I help?”); 20 = least of it.
criterion_descriptions.slop_index: Inverted — penalizes clichés and filler (“I'm here for you”); 20 = freshest, least generic.

scoring_method.name: Bradley-Terry (weighted, mean-anchored)
scoring_method.summary: The Elo ladder is a weighted Bradley-Terry fit, not an extrapolation from hand-picked reference models. For each unordered model pair, scenario, and criterion, the judge's signed margins from both A/B orderings are averaged into a net preference. The sign picks the winner and the absolute value is the observation's weight, so a decisive win counts more than a narrow one; an exact tie splits half-credit to each side. A pure-Python Bradley-Terry MM solver turns those weighted comparisons into one log-strength per model.
scoring_method.scaling: Each model's log-strength is centered on the field mean and scaled by 400/ln(10), then added to 1500 — the same spread convention as chess Elo, but anchored to the field's own mean instead of two synthetic reference points. No model is pinned to a fixed number; the numbers move only relative to each other.
scoring_method.confidence_interval: The 95% interval next to each Elo is a bootstrap over scenarios: the scenario set is resampled with replacement 200 times, the whole ladder is re-solved each time, and the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of a model's normalized Elo are its interval. When two models' intervals overlap, the gap between them is within noise.

cross_judge_cost_caveat: Judging cost (judging_cost_usd) is only comparable within a single judge model/generation. Different judge models use different tokenizers for the same transcript text (e.g. Claude Sonnet 5 counts roughly 30% more tokens than Sonnet 4.6), so cross-judge cost figures are NOT apples-to-apples.
